title: Ustekinumab -- Indications
content: Ustekinumab is a human monoclonal antibody typically used to treat moderate to severe plaque psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, moderate to severe Crohn disease, or moderate to severe ulcerative colitis (inflammatory bowel disease). Ustekinumab mediates the body's T-cell response by acting as an antagonist against interleukin-12 (IL12) and interleukin-23 (IL23). The drug is FDA approved for use in moderate to severe plaque psoriasis (Ps), particularly in those patients who are candidates for phototherapy or systemic therapy, aged 6 or older. Patients with active psoriatic arthritis (PsA) may use ustekinumab as monotherapy or in combination with methotrexate.

title: Ustekinumab -- Continuing Education Activity
content: Ustekinumab is a medication used to manage and treat a variety of inflammatory conditions, including psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, and inflammatory bowel disease. It is in the monoclonal antibody class of medications. This activity reviews the indications, action, and contraindications for ustekinumab as a valuable agent in treating inflammatory diseases. This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, and other key factors (e.g., off-label uses, dosing, pharmacodynamics, pharmacokinetics, monitoring, relevant interactions) pertinent for members of the interprofessional team in the management of psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, and inflammatory bowel disease.

title: Ustekinumab -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
content: The management of inflammatory diseases such as psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, and inflammatory bowel disease is complex and associated with a high-cost burden. The rational and cost-effective use of biological medications, such as ustekinumab, is a key component in managing these diseases. To achieve the desired outcome, an interprofessional healthcare team approach is necessary. Internal medicine clinicians (including mid-level practitioners such as nurse practitioners and physician assistants) may work with specialists, including rheumatologists, gastroenterologists, or dermatologists, to confirm the diagnosis and decide on a treatment course. Nurses facilitate the preparation and administration of medications, as well as monitoring patients after use; they should immediately report any concerns to the ordering/prescribing clinician. Pharmacists can ensure no relevant drug-drug interactions exist, evaluate the relevance of contraindications to treatment, verify dosing is correct (especially inasmuch as ustekinumab utilizes weight-based dosing regimens), and ensure the appropriateness of prescriptions. All clinical personnel need to participate in patient counseling, educate the patient regarding potential adverse events, signs of therapeutic failure, and provide access to resources and points of contact should they need to reach out to the interprofessional team. Social workers identify barriers to compliance and provide resources to patients. Each member of the interprofessional team's contributions can provide expertise from their discipline to help drive a positive outcome for patients with potentially fewer adverse events and improve the effectiveness of the broader healthcare system. [Level 5]

title: Ustekinumab -- Contraindications
content: As with all biological medications, appropriate screening for severe infection must be carried out before beginning a drug such as ustekinumab; this includes screening for infections such as tuberculosis, HIV, hepatitis B, and hepatitis C and treatment if identified. Providers must exercise caution in recommending treatment for elderly patients, patients with a history of malignancy, or a predisposition to infection. [29]

title: Brodalumab -- Indications
content: Phase 3 studies comparing brodalumab with ustekinumab showed that at standard dosing, brodalumab was able to achieve higher rates of PASI 100, 44%, and 37% versus 22% and 19% of patients treated with ustekinumab. Brodalumab is also more effective than ustekinumab in biologic-experienced patients: 40% of patients previously treated with a biologic medication achieved PASI 100, while only 17% of biologic-experienced patients treated with ustekinumab were able to achieve the same level of efficacy. Additionally, brodalumab had a more rapid onset of action with PASI 75 responders reaching this benchmark in a median of 4.2 weeks compared to 9.4 weeks for ustekinumab. [4] [5]

title: Generalized Pustular Psoriasis -- Treatment / Management
content: Second-line therapies for GPP include biologic agents such as etanercept, adalimumab, ustekinumab, and secukinumab, as well as topical treatments, particularly corticosteroids, calcipotriene, and tacrolimus, for localized involvement of the palms and soles. [28] A small case series reported clinical improvement with bimekizumab in patients with palmoplantar pustulosis and palmoplantar plaque psoriasis presenting with pustules. [29] Formal treatment guidelines for 2nd-line therapies are lacking. Anecdotal evidence has described paradoxical induction of pustular psoriasis with certain biologics, highlighting the need for further evaluation and consensus. [30]

title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1598
content: Antiflogistika, a class of anti‑inflammatory agents, encompasses several corticosteroid drugs that share the common function of reducing inflammation in various clinical settings. Deflazacort, prednison, fluocinonid, diflorasone, halcinonid, and triamcinolonacetonid are all members of the antiflogistika group. Each of these compounds exerts anti‑inflammatory effects through glucocorticoid receptor activation, leading to suppression of pro‑inflammatory cytokines and immune cell activity. Recognizing that these medications belong to the same therapeutic class helps clinicians select appropriate anti‑inflammatory therapy, anticipate similar side‑effect profiles, and manage dosing strategies across conditions such as allergic reactions, autoimmune disorders, and dermatologic inflammation.

title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2127
content: Immunosuppressants constitute a diverse group of agents that dampen immune activity and are employed in transplantation, autoimmune diseases, and other conditions requiring immune modulation. This class includes inhibitory interleukin compounds, the antimetabolite mycophenolate and its pro‑drug mycophenolate mofetil, antilymphocyte serum, azaserin, the monoclonal antibodies basiliximab and daclizumab, the mTOR inhibitor everolimus, and the fungal toxin gliotoxin. Additional members are cyclophosphamide (koformycin), mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová), the glucocorticoid paramethasone, and the calcineurin inhibitor tacrolimus. These drugs act through various mechanisms—such as blocking interleukin signaling, inhibiting nucleotide synthesis, antagonizing the IL‑2 receptor, inhibiting mTOR signaling, or modulating glucocorticoid and calcineurin pathways—to achieve immunosuppression and improve patient outcomes in settings where controlling the immune response is critical.

title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1400
content: Immunosuppressants represent a diverse pharmacologic class that includes antilymphocyte serum, azaserine, cyclosporine, daclizumab, ellipticins, everolimus, gliotoxin, mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová), muromonab‑CD3, and tacrolimus (takrolimus). Within this class, inhibitory interleukin agents are considered a subordinate subgroup (“has child”). These agents act to dampen immune responses and are employed in conditions requiring suppression of lymphocyte activity, such as organ transplantation, autoimmune diseases, and certain inflammatory disorders. However, the use of immunosuppressants is contraindicated in the setting of human herpesvirus 1 (HSV‑1) infection, reflecting the heightened risk of viral reactivation when immune function is deliberately reduced. Recognizing both the therapeutic breadth of immunosuppressants and their specific contraindications is essential for safe patient management and optimal clinical outcomes.

title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 381
content: Immunosuppressants comprise a diverse group of agents that are used to dampen immune activity in settings such as organ transplantation, autoimmune disorders, and inflammatory diseases. This class includes inhibitory interleukin agents, the antimetabolite mycophenolate (also known as mycophenolic acid) and its pro‑drug mycophenolate mofetil (including its hydrochloride form), as well as azaserin, basiliximab, cyclosporine, everolimus, gliotoxin, muromonab‑CD3, and tacrolimus. Mycophenolate and mycophenolic acid function by blocking purine synthesis in lymphocytes, whereas cyclosporine and tacrolimus inhibit calcineurin, preventing activation of T‑cell transcription factors. Everolimus exerts its effect through inhibition of the mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) pathway, limiting cell proliferation. Basiliximab and muromonab‑CD3 are monoclonal antibodies that target specific surface receptors on activated T cells, providing targeted immunosuppression. Gliotoxin, a fungal toxin, also possesses immunosuppressive properties through interference with cytokine signaling. Collectively, these agents illustrate the multiple mechanistic pathways—interleukin inhibition, antimetabolite activity, calcineurin blockade, mTOR inhibition, and monoclonal antibody‑mediated receptor blockade—by which immunosuppressants achieve therapeutic immune modulation, underscoring their central role in contemporary patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 687
content: Immunosuppressants represent a broad class of agents that dampen immune activity through a variety of mechanisms and are employed in transplantation, autoimmune disorders, and other conditions requiring immune modulation. The group includes inhibitory interleukin agents, antimetabolites such as mycophenolate, mycophenolate mofetil, mycophenolate mofetil hydrochloride, mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová), azaserin, and koformycin, which interfere with nucleotide synthesis and lymphocyte proliferation. Calcineurin inhibitors, exemplified by cyklosporiny (cyclosporine) and takrolimus (tacrolimus), block T‑cell activation by inhibiting calcineurin signaling. The mTOR pathway is targeted by everolimus, providing another route to suppress cell growth and immune responses. Monoclonal antibodies and biologic agents such as basiliximab, daklizumab (daclizumab), muromonab‑CD3, and elipticiny act on specific surface receptors or cytokine pathways to prevent lymphocyte activation. Polyclonal preparations like antilymfocytární sérum (antilymphocyte serum) also contribute to immunosuppression by broadly neutralizing immune cells. Glucocorticoids, including meprednisone and paramethason (paramethasone), exert anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects by altering gene transcription. Additionally, gliotoxin, a fungal toxin, possesses immunosuppressive properties through inhibition of NF‑κB and other signaling cascades. Together, these diverse agents illustrate the multiple biochemical targets—interleukin signaling, calcineurin, mTOR, nucleotide synthesis, surface receptors, and transcriptional pathways—through which immune function can be attenuated, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate regimens and managing associated risks.

content: Immunosuppressants constitute a broad pharmacologic class that includes a diverse array of agents used to attenuate immune system activity in settings such as organ transplantation, autoimmune disorders, and inflammatory diseases. Within this class are inhibitory interleukinu compounds, which target interleukin pathways to reduce immune activation, and mycophenolate, a purine synthesis inhibitor that limits lymphocyte proliferation. Antilymfocytární sérum, a polyclonal antibody preparation, also belongs to the immunosuppressant group, as do azaserin and basiliximab, both of which interfere with T‑cell activation. Cyklosporiny (cyclosporine) and takrolimus (tacrolimus) are calcineurin inhibitors that suppress cytokine production, while daklizumab (daclizumab) blocks the IL‑2 receptor to prevent T‑cell expansion. Additional members include elipticiny, gliotoxin, and the pro‑drug hydrochlorid mykofenolátu mofetilu (mycophenolate mofetil hydrochloride), which is metabolized to the active agent mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová). Koformycin, a nucleoside analog, and muromonab‑CD3, a monoclonal antibody directed against the CD3 complex, further expand the repertoire of immunosuppressive therapies. Collectively, these agents illustrate the extensive range of mechanisms—such as cytokine inhibition, lymphocyte depletion, and signal transduction blockade—through which immunosuppressants achieve therapeutic immune modulation, underscoring their critical role in contemporary patient care.

This grouping indicates that each of these agents is used systemically to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). The list includes several monoclonal antibodies—benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab—that target specific inflammatory pathways (e.g., interleukin‑5 or thymic stromal lymphopoietin) to reduce airway inflammation, as well as roflumilast, a phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibitor that helps control inflammation and bronchoconstriction. title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1722
content: Immunosuppressants are a class of immunologic factors that act to dampen or inhibit the activity of the immune system. Within this class, several agents are identified as direct “children,” including inhibitory interleukin agents, mycophenolate, and mycophenolate mofetil (mykofenolát mofetil).
